title: Listening - Part 3: Deep Sea Exploration
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two marine explorers, Ben Taylor and Dr. Maya Rossi, about their journey to the bottom of the Mariana Trench. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview about the physical and scientific challenges of exploring the deepest parts of the ocean.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Today we’re talking to Ben Taylor and Dr. Maya Rossi, who have recently returned from a record-breaking dive to the Challenger Deep. Ben, the pressure down there is equivalent to an elephant standing on your thumb. How did that feel inside the submersible?

Ben: Strangely, you don't feel the pressure itself. The hull is made of specialized titanium, so inside, it’s just like sitting in a very cramped office. [15]What got to me was the utter stillness; the realization that you are effectively on another planet[/15], miles away from any hope of rescue. It’s more of a mental weight than a physical one.

Interviewer: Maya, you were there to look for new life forms. Did you find anything that defied your expectations?

Maya: Every single dive does. But this time, [16]I was struck by the discovery of complex organisms thriving near hydrothermal vents that weren't dependent on sunlight at all[/16]. We knew it was possible, but seeing it in person—the sheer density of life in a place that should be a desert—was life-changing for a biologist. It reshapes our understanding of where life might exist in the universe.

Interviewer: Ben, you had a technical glitch halfway down. That must have been terrifying.

Ben: We lost communication with the surface for about twenty minutes. [17]I was actually quite frustrated because it meant we had to pause our scientific readings[/17] while we rebooted the system. Most people assume I’d be panicking about oxygen, but the sub is built for that. I just wanted to get back to work because every minute on the bottom is incredibly expensive.

Interviewer: Maya, the deep ocean is often called the 'final frontier'. Is it better to send robots instead of humans?

Maya: Robots are great for mapping, but [18]I believe the human eye can spot nuances in behavior and geology that a camera lens simply misses[/18]. There is a level of intuitive decision-making that happens when you're looking through a porthole that we haven't been able to program into an AI yet. We need both.

Interviewer: Ben, you’ve seen the impact of humanity even at 11,000 metres. What did you find?

Ben: It was heartbreaking. [19]The sight of a plastic bag floating past the window at the deepest point on Earth was a wake-up call for the whole team.[/19] It’s a stark reminder that our waste reaches places we haven't even named yet. We aren't just exploring; we're witnessing the footprint of a global crisis.

Interviewer: What’s next for the team? Are there more trenches to conquer?

Maya: There are, but [20]our focus now is on analyzing the thousands of soil samples we brought back to look for new antibiotics.[/20] The deep sea is a chemical goldmine. We want to prove that protecting these areas isn't just about 'saving the whales', it's about the future of human medicine. Ben and I are already planning the next phase, but it will be in the lab, not the ocean.

questions.question: How did Ben feel during the descent to the Challenger Deep?
options: A) overwhelmed by the physical pressure on the hull
B) comfortable in the spacious interior of the sub
C) affected by the sense of isolation from the world
D) confident that a rescue mission would be successful
questions.explanation: Ben says what 'got to him' was the stillness and being 'miles away from any hope of rescue'.

questions.question: What was the most significant finding for Maya on this dive?
options: A) the fact that life cannot exist without sunlight
B) the amount of life found in a normally barren area
C) the desert-like conditions of the hydrothermal vents
D) the realization that the universe is empty of life
questions.explanation: She was struck by the 'sheer density of life in a place that should be a desert'.

questions.question: Ben's reaction to the communication failure was mainly one of
options: A) intense fear for his personal safety.
B) annoyance at the interruption of his data collection.
C) concern about the dwindling oxygen levels.
D) relief that he could finally take a break.
questions.explanation: Ben explicitly says he was 'quite frustrated' because they had to 'pause our scientific readings'.

questions.question: Why does Maya prefer human exploration over robotic exploration?
options: A) Robots are incapable of mapping the seabed correctly.
B) Camera lenses are too expensive to use in the deep sea.
C) Humans can make better intuitive judgments on-site.
D) AI has already been perfectly programmed for this task.
questions.explanation: She believes the human eye and 'intuitive decision-making' are superior to current AI and cameras.

questions.question: The discovery of a plastic bag at the bottom of the trench
options: A) showed that the team had reached the wrong location.
B) was an expected part of the mission's findings.
C) highlighted the extent of global pollution.
D) suggested that humans had lived there before.
questions.explanation: Ben calls it a 'wake-up call' and a 'stark reminder' of a global crisis.

questions.question: The primary goal of Maya's future research is to
options: A) find more trenches to explore with Ben.
B) develop new types of marine conservation.
C) identify medicinal properties in sea samples.
D) live in the ocean for an extended period.
questions.explanation: She states that their focus is now on looking for 'new antibiotics' in the soil samples.
